edge: Human teammates form conscious and subconscious expectations of each other during interaction.
Teaming success is contingent on whether expectations can be met.
For an intelligent agent to operate beside a human, it must consider the human's expectations of its behavior.
Disregarding human's expectations can lead to loss of trust and degraded team performance.
Key challenge is that human's expectation may not align with agent's optimal behavior.
Prior work on explicable planning described ability of agents to respect their human teammate's expectations.
EPS significantly extends ability to stochastic domains in a reinforcement learning setting.
In contrast to traditional RL methods, EPS must infer human's hidden expectations.
Inferences require information about human's belief about the domain dynamics.
Information can be encoded by a surrogate reward function for EPS.

Humans provide feedback on the agent's behavior which is used to reshape the agent's reward function. The success of collaboration between humans and agents depends on whether expectations can be met.
The surrogate reward function is used to reshape the agent's reward function and is equivalent to searching for an explicable policy.
EPS is evaluated in navigation domains with synthetic human models and in an autonomous driving domain with a user study.

EPS is evaluated in an autonomous driving domain with a user study.
The method can generate explicable behaviors that reconcile task performance with human expectations intelligently and has real-world relevance in human-agent teaming domains.

explicable policy search (EPS) significantly extends explicable planning to stochastic domains in a reinforcement learning setting with continuous state and action spaces.
A practical solution for explicable policy search is proposed by introducing a surrogate reward function learned from human feedback data.
explicable policy search method is evaluated extensively with simulations and human subjects.
explainable behavior generation differs from interpretable behavior generation as it aims to generate behaviors that are inherently and implicitly interpretable to humans.
Interpretable behavior generation focuses on generating behaviors under interpretable model representations so that their rationales can be explicitly conveyed.

Behavior generated by the latter may not be interpretable by itself but an explainable behavior will be. Legibility is about generating trajectories that better reveal the underlying goal of the agent.

An explainable behavior will be desirable for domains where explicit communication should be used sparingly. Explicability is one of the terms introduced for different aspects of explainable behavior generation.
Legibility and explicability are terms related to explainable behavior generation.
Legible motion planning is about generating trajectories that better reveal the underlying goal of the agent.

Explicable planning aims at generating plans that better align with the human’s expectation given the goal, while legible motion planning is about generating trajectories that better reveal the underlying goal of the agent.
Explicable planning methods revolve around the idea of model reconciliation where an agent makes decisions based on two different models instead of one.

Prior work focused on training value-aligned learning agents that learn to respect different reward models, but do not consider the differences in domain dynamics, which is a key characterization of explicable planning methods.
Value-aligned learning agents learn to respect different reward models, but do not consider the differences in the domain dynamics.
The problem of explicable planning is addressed in a classical planning setting, such as PDDL.

edge: The challenge is to extend the problem formulation from deterministic domains to stochastic domains with continuous state and action spaces.
In this context, the user's reward model may differ from the agent's reward model, but it is assumed that this difference does not affect the agent's perception of the 'goal'.
The problem setting of legible motion planning involves considering multiple candidate goals.
Estimating the human's expectation requires learning both the human's belief about the domain dynamics and her reward model based on human feedback.
Existing work on inverse reinforcement learning and reward learning can learn the reward model from human data.
Learning about the domain dynamics and the human's reward model is based on human feedback.
The human's inaccurate belief about the domain dynamics may skew the human's feedback and lead to learning opposite preferences with respect to the human's true reward model.
Policy learning methods that use human feedback, such as reward shaping, policy shaping, and interactive RL, may face challenges when the human has an inaccurate belief about the domain dynamics.
Human's belief about the domain dynamics is tightly coupled in the human's feedback and should be jointly learned.
Joint learning is challenging due to its high dimensionality, which makes it impractical for real-world domains.
InfoGAIL uses latent code to discover salient semantic features to accommodate demonstrations from different experts.
Differences in the domain dynamics are captured by the latent code.
Our problem may be viewed as a form of off-dynamics learning that addresses transfer learning from a source to a target domain.
We do not have direct access to the target domain in EPS, which is hidden in the human's mind.

Assuming the two MDPs share the same S, A, ρ, and γ, one is the agent’s model MA with the true domain dynamics TA and the engineered reward function rA, and the other is the human’s model of expectation MHA and the human’s reward function rH. A trajectory is likely to be generated by the human’s model when it is associated with a high probability in the model.
For a policy to be the most explicable, a trajectory with a given probability in the human’s model should also appear with the same probability in MA.
The expectation over the distribution of trajectories is considered as the explicability score.
Evaluating the explicability of a policy requires considering two models and their contrastive aspects.
The explicability score is evaluated by analyzing the closeness of two distributions, which is a key aspect in determining the explicability of a policy.
The policy explicability score is specifically designed for stochastic environments and differs from scores used in deterministic domains.
Policy explicability score extends the concept of explicability scores to consider plan distributions, highlighting different aspects of explicability.
An agent striving to maximize the policy explicability score would aim to closely replicate human expectations in behavior.
The policy explicability score involves comparing distributions of agent and human trajectories, with a close match resulting in a score of 0.
Equation (1) represents the objective of maximizing the policy explicability score by adjusting the policy parameters.
pA(τ ) and pH(τ) refer to the distributions of trajectories under the agent and human models respectively, which are crucial in computing the policy explicability score.

We used a simulated autonomous driving domain [29] to evaluate our method with human subjects and demonstrate its real-world relevance.
The state space is featured by the position and velocity of the ego-vehicle and nearby vehicles.
The action space consists of five discrete actions.
The autonomous driving vehicle is running on the highway with a car in front of it on the same lane and with the same speed.
One common type of cognitive bias is the availability bias, which reflects humans’ tendency to overestimate the likelihood of events with greater availability in memory.
Such biases can occur in driving since we regularly drive under familiar conditions.
These conditions are mentioned in the same sentence and are related to the vehicle's domain dynamics becoming more stochastic due to slippery roads.

Sleety condition affects the vehicle's domain dynamics as they become more stochastic due to slippery roads.
The vehicle's domain dynamics affected by slippery roads are related to the availability bias influencing human decisions under similar conditions.

The evaluation showed that the approach could better handle situations under model differences.
The approach contributed a critical tool to achieving explainable human-agent interaction.
The assumptions underlying our work include that the human generates expectation based on her objective and belief about the domain dynamics.
It would be useful to consider situations where such assumptions do not hold.
In scenarios where inaccuracies stem from intrinsic cognitive biases, it is a reasonable assumption.
In cases where discrepancies were due to information asymmetries, the belief can change dynamically and needs to be actively monitored.
Other possible directions include more complex reconciliation of the expected return and policy explicability score.
Weighted policy explicability scores can be used to incorporate complex reconciliation.
There is a relationship between policy explicability score and weighted policy explicability scores as the latter incorporates trajectory similarity.
EPS may sometimes cause user preferences to be ignored, which is necessary in many scenarios.
EPS combines the surrogate reward with an engineered reward from the agent’s designer to maintain critical features while respecting user preferences.
EPS combines the surrogate reward with an engineered reward to respect user preferences.
There is a difference between human understanding and ground truth in real-world scenarios.
The combination of surrogate reward and engineered reward should not be replaced by the user’s reward even if it can be perfectly estimated due to human biases and misunderstandings.
Real-world deployment of EPS may require interactive design to explain to the user why her preferences are ignored.
The research is fundamental in nature without notable negative societal impacts.
The approach may be used to misguide RL agents to ignore human preferences in adversarial settings, which requires further investigation.
